auth.patDesc: For GitLab and Gitee — generate a token from your provider settings and add it in AgentsMesh. Requires repo read/write scope.

podAssociation.title: Repository & Pod Association
podAssociation.description: When creating a Pod with a repository:
podAssociation.step1: The Runner clones the repository (if not already cached)
podAssociation.step2: A new Git worktree is created for the Pod
podAssociation.step3: The AI agent starts in the worktree directory
podAssociation.step4: Changes are isolated from other Pods working on the same repo
podAssociation.step5: When the Pod terminates, the worktree is cleaned up

worktree.title: Git Worktree Isolation
worktree.description: Git worktrees allow multiple Pods to work on the same repository simultaneously. Each Pod operates on its own branch without affecting others. This is critical for parallel AI agent development where multiple agents may modify the same codebase.

management.title: Managing Repositories
management.addRepoSteps: Go to Settings → Repositories → Add Repository. Select your Git provider, authenticate, and choose the repositories to connect.
management.removeRepoSteps: Removing a repository disconnects it from AgentsMesh. Existing Pods using this repository will not be affected, but new Pods cannot be created with it.

setup.title: Runner Setup
setup.description: Runners are the execution environments for AI agent Pods. Set up a runner on any machine with Git and your preferred development tools installed.

requirements.title: System Requirements
requirements.item5: At least 4GB RAM (8GB+ recommended for multiple Pods)

quickInstall.title: Quick Installation
quickInstall.methodToken: Option A: Register with a token
quickInstall.methodLogin: Option B: Register with browser login
quickInstall.tokenHint: Get your registration token from Settings → Runners → Create Token in the AgentsMesh web interface.

registrationToken.title: Creating a Registration Token
registrationToken.step1: Go to Settings → Runners in the web interface
registrationToken.step3: Set an optional description and expiration
registrationToken.step5: Use the token when configuring your runner
registrationToken.securityNote: Security Note: Registration tokens are one-time use. Once a runner registers, it receives mTLS certificates for secure communication. You can revoke certificates from the runner settings if needed.

verifying.title: Verifying Installation
verifying.description: After starting the runner, verify it's connected:
verifying.step1: Go to Settings → Runners in the web interface
verifying.step2: Find your runner in the list — it should show ● Online
verifying.step3: Try creating an AgentPod using this runner

troubleshooting.title: Troubleshooting
troubleshooting.offlineDesc: Check network connectivity to the AgentsMesh server. Ensure firewalls allow outbound gRPC connections (port 9443 for mTLS).
troubleshooting.podsFailDesc: Verify Git is installed and configured. Check that the runner has access to clone repositories (SSH keys or tokens configured).
troubleshooting.tokenInvalidDesc: Registration tokens are single-use. Create a new token from Settings → Runners if the original was already used.

mcpTools.title: MCP Tools
mcpTools.description: The Runner provides 26 MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools for AI agents to collaborate with other agents. Tools are organized into 7 categories: Discovery, Terminal, Binding, Channel, Ticket, Pod, and Loop.

overview.title: Overview
overview.description: MCP tools are automatically available to AI agents running in AgentPod. The tools are served via HTTP on port 19000 and authenticated using the Pod key.
overview.autoConfig: Automatic Configuration: When using Claude Code, the runner automatically generates the MCP configuration file with the correct URL and headers.

discovery.title: Discovery Tools
discovery.description: Tools for discovering available resources in the mesh.
discovery.listPodsDesc: List other Pods available for collaboration

binding.title: Pod Binding Tools
binding.getBindingsDesc: Get all bindings for this Pod
binding.getBoundPodsDesc: Get Pods bound to this Pod

loop.title: Loop Tools
loop.description: Tools for managing automated loops — repeatable tasks that can be triggered manually or via cron scheduling.
loop.listLoopsDesc: List automated loops in the organization

pod.title: Pod Tools
pod.description: Tools for creating, observing, and controlling Pods. Observation and input tools require an active binding with appropriate scopes.
pod.getPodSnapshotDesc: Get a snapshot of another Pod's terminal output (requires pod:read)
pod.sendPodInputDesc: Send text and/or key presses to another Pod (requires pod:write)
pod.getPodStatusDesc: Get the agent execution status of a Pod (executing/waiting/idle)

gitIntegration.title: Git Integration
gitIntegration.description: A complete guide to connecting Git providers and using repositories with AgentsMesh.

overview.title: Overview
overview.description: AgentsMesh integrates with popular Git hosting platforms to provide seamless repository access for AI agents. This guide covers the end-to-end setup process.

github.title: GitHub (OAuth)
github.description: Connect your GitHub account using OAuth for one-click authorization:
github.step1: Go to Settings → Personal → Git Settings
github.step3: Authorize AgentsMesh in the GitHub popup
github.step4: Select which repositories to grant access to
github.step5: Your repositories will appear in Settings → Repositories

gitlab.title: GitLab (Personal Access Token)
gitlab.description: Connect GitLab using a Personal Access Token:
gitlab.step1: In GitLab, go to Settings → Access Tokens
gitlab.step2: Create a new token with api and read_repository scopes
gitlab.step3: In AgentsMesh, go to Settings → Personal → Git Settings
gitlab.step4: Click Add GitLab Provider and paste your token
gitlab.step5: Enter your GitLab instance URL (for self-hosted)
gitlab.selfHosted: Self-Hosted GitLab: Enter the full URL of your GitLab instance (e.g., https://gitlab.yourcompany.com).

gitee.title: Gitee (Personal Access Token)
gitee.description: Connect Gitee using a Personal Access Token:
gitee.step1: In Gitee, go to Settings → Personal Access Tokens
gitee.step2: Create a new token with projects scope
gitee.step3: In AgentsMesh, go to Settings → Personal → Git Settings
gitee.step4: Click Add Gitee Provider and paste your token

sshKeys.title: SSH Key Management
sshKeys.description: For repositories accessed via SSH, ensure your Runner has the correct SSH keys configured:
sshKeys.step1: Generate an SSH key pair on your Runner machine
sshKeys.step2: Add the public key to your Git provider (GitHub/GitLab/Gitee)
sshKeys.step3: The Runner will use the SSH key when cloning repositories
sshKeys.dockerNote: Docker Runners: Mount your SSH keys as a read-only volume: -v ~/.ssh:/root/.ssh:ro

podUsage.title: Using Repositories in Pods
podUsage.description: Once a repository is connected, you can link it to Pods:
podUsage.step1: When creating a new Pod, select a repository from the dropdown
podUsage.step2: The Runner clones the repo and creates an isolated Git worktree
podUsage.step3: The AI agent starts in the repository's working directory
podUsage.step4: All changes are isolated to the Pod's worktree

teamManagement.title: Team Management
teamManagement.description: Learn how to set up and manage your team in AgentsMesh.

overview.title: Overview
overview.description: AgentsMesh uses Organizations as the primary unit for team collaboration. Each organization has its own members, runners, repositories, and settings.

createOrg.title: Creating an Organization
createOrg.description: When you first register, you'll be prompted to create an organization. You can also create additional organizations later:
createOrg.step1: Click your profile menu → Create Organization
createOrg.step2: Enter an organization name and display name
createOrg.step4: Your organization is ready to use

inviteMembers.title: Inviting Members
inviteMembers.description: Invite team members to collaborate in your organization:
inviteMembers.step5: The invitee receives an email with a join link
inviteMembers.note: Invited users who don't have an AgentsMesh account will be prompted to register first.

roles.title: Roles & Permissions
roles.ownerDesc: Full access to all settings, billing, and member management. Can delete the organization.
roles.adminDesc: Can manage members, runners, repositories, and settings. Cannot delete the organization or manage billing.
roles.memberDesc: Can create Pods, use runners, access repositories, and manage tickets. Cannot change organization settings.

orgSettings.title: Organization Settings
orgSettings.general: General — Organization name, display name, and avatar
orgSettings.members: Members — Invite, remove, and manage member roles
orgSettings.runners: Runners — View and manage connected runners
orgSettings.providers: AI Providers — API keys for AI services

multiAgent.title: Multi-Agent Workflows
multiAgent.description: Learn how to orchestrate multiple AI agents to collaborate on complex development tasks.

overview.title: Overview
overview.description: Multi-agent workflows allow you to split complex tasks across multiple AI agents. Each agent runs in its own Pod, and they communicate through Channels and Pod Bindings.

setup.title: Setting Up a Multi-Agent Workflow
setup.step1: Create a Channel for the task (e.g., 'feature-auth-implementation')
setup.step2: Launch multiple Pods, each with a different role or focus
setup.step3: Link all Pods to the same Channel
setup.step4: Each Pod uses MCP tools to communicate and coordinate
setup.step5: Monitor progress through the Mesh topology view

scenario1.title: Scenario: Code Review Workflow
scenario1.description: Use two agents — one writes code, another reviews it:
scenario1.developer: Developer Pod — Writes code based on ticket requirements
scenario1.reviewer: Reviewer Pod — Observes the developer's terminal output via binding, reviews changes, and provides feedback through the Channel
scenario1.flow: The reviewer can use get_pod_snapshot to watch the developer work, then send feedback via the Channel. The developer reads the feedback and iterates.

scenario2.title: Scenario: Frontend + Backend Split
scenario2.description: Parallelize full-stack development with two specialized agents:
scenario2.frontend: Frontend Pod — Works on UI components, styles, and client logic
scenario2.backend: Backend Pod — Implements API endpoints, database models, and business logic
scenario2.coordination: Both Pods share a Channel where they coordinate API contracts, data schemas, and integration points. They can use the shared document to maintain an API specification.

scenario3.title: Scenario: Test & Development in Parallel
scenario3.description: Run testing alongside development for faster iteration:
scenario3.testPod: Testing Pod — Writes and runs tests for the new code
scenario3.process: The testing Pod monitors the development Pod's progress via Channel messages. When the developer signals completion of a module, the tester writes and executes tests, reporting results back through the Channel.

bestPractices.title: Best Practices
bestPractices.tip1: Start with clear task assignments — Give each Pod a focused initial prompt
bestPractices.tip2: Use Channels for coordination — Prefer Channel messages over direct terminal access
bestPractices.tip3: Link to tickets — Associate Pods with tickets for automatic context
bestPractices.tip4: Monitor via Mesh — Use the topology view to track collaboration status
bestPractices.tip5: Clean up — Terminate Pods when tasks are complete to free resources

overview.title: API Overview
overview.description: Integrate third-party services with AgentsMesh using organization-level API Keys. Automate pod management, ticket tracking, and more through the REST API.

overview.title: Overview
overview.description: AgentsMesh provides organization-level API Keys for third-party integrations, CI/CD pipelines, and automation workflows. With API Keys you can:
overview.item2: Automate ticket creation and status updates

gettingKey.title: Getting an API Key
gettingKey.description: API Keys are created by organization Owners or Admins from the web interface.
gettingKey.step1: Navigate to Settings → Organization → API Keys
gettingKey.step3: Enter a name, optional expiration date, and select the scopes you need
gettingKey.step4: Copy the generated key (starts with amk_)
gettingKey.warning: Important: The API Key is only displayed once at creation time. Store it securely — you cannot retrieve it later.

basePath.title: Base URL & Path
basePath.description: All external API endpoints follow this pattern:
basePath.slugNote: Replace '{'slug'}' with your organization's slug (found in Settings → Organization).

authentication.title: Authentication
authentication.description: Learn how to authenticate your API requests using API Keys.

methods.title: Authentication Methods
methods.description: Include your API Key in every request using one of the following methods:
methods.headerMethodDesc: Pass the key directly in the X-API-Key header. This is the recommended method.
methods.bearerMethodDesc: Alternatively, pass the key as a Bearer token in the Authorization header.

scopes.title: Scopes
scopes.description: Each API Key is assigned one or more scopes that determine which endpoints it can access. Write scopes implicitly include read access for the same resource.
scopes.podsWriteEndpoints: Create pods, terminate pods + all read endpoints
scopes.ticketsReadEndpoints: List tickets, get ticket, get board
scopes.ticketsWriteEndpoints: Create, update, delete tickets + all read endpoints
scopes.channelsReadEndpoints: List channels, get channel, list messages
scopes.channelsWriteEndpoints: Create/update channels, send messages + all read endpoints
scopes.runnersReadEndpoints: List runners, get runner, list available runners, list runner pods
scopes.reposReadEndpoints: List repositories, get repository, list branches, list merge requests

errors.title: Error Handling
errors.description: The API returns standard HTTP status codes. Common error responses:
errors.badRequest: Bad Request — Invalid parameters or request body
errors.unauthorized: Unauthorized — Missing or invalid API Key
errors.forbidden: Forbidden — API Key lacks required scope for this endpoint
errors.notFound: Not Found — Resource does not exist or organization slug is invalid

pods.title: Pods API
pods.description: Manage AgentPods programmatically. Create, list, and terminate Pods through the REST API.

listPods.title: GET /pods
listPods.description: Retrieve a paginated list of pods in the organization. Supports filtering by status.

params.status: Filter by pod status (e.g., running, terminated)
params.limit: Maximum number of results to return
params.offset: Number of results to skip for pagination

getPod.title: GET /pods/:key
getPod.description: Retrieve detailed information about a specific pod by its unique key.

params.key: The unique key identifier of the pod

createPod.title: POST /pods
createPod.description: Create a new AgentPod on a specified runner. The pod will be initialized with the given configuration and start executing.

fields.runner_id: ID of the runner to create the pod on
fields.prompt: Initial prompt or task for the agent
fields.repository_id: Repository to associate with the pod
fields.channel_id: Channel to associate with the pod
fields.custom_agent_command: Custom command to launch the agent
fields.custom_agent_args: Arguments for the custom agent command
fields.auto_close: Whether the pod should auto-close after task completion
fields.pod_timeout_minutes: Maximum lifetime of the pod in minutes

terminatePod.title: POST /pods/:key/terminate
terminatePod.description: Terminate a running pod. The pod process will be stopped and resources released.

params.key: The unique key identifier of the pod to terminate

tickets.title: Tickets API
tickets.description: Automate ticket management. Create, update, and track tickets through the REST API.

listTickets.title: GET /tickets
listTickets.description: Retrieve a paginated list of tickets. Supports filtering by repository, status, assignee, and labels.

params.status: Filter by status (backlog, todo, in_progress, in_review, done)
params.limit: Maximum number of results to return
params.offset: Number of results to skip for pagination

getBoard.title: GET /tickets/board
getBoard.description: Retrieve the Kanban board view with tickets organized into columns by status.

getTicket.title: GET /tickets/:slug
getTicket.description: Retrieve detailed information about a specific ticket by its human-readable slug.

createTicket.title: POST /tickets
createTicket.description: Create a new ticket. Requires a title at minimum.

fields.title: Ticket title (1-500 characters)
fields.priority: Priority level: low, medium, high, or urgent
fields.assignee_id: User ID to assign the ticket to

updateTicket.title: PUT /tickets/:slug
updateTicket.description: Update an existing ticket. All fields are optional — only provided fields will be updated.

updateStatus.title: PATCH /tickets/:slug/status
updateStatus.description: Update only the status of a ticket. This is a convenience endpoint for status transitions.

fields.status: New status: backlog, todo, in_progress, in_review, or done

deleteTicket.title: DELETE /tickets/:slug
deleteTicket.description: Permanently delete a ticket. This action cannot be undone.

loops.title: Loops API
loops.description: Manage automated Loop tasks and their runs. Create Loops, configure triggers, and monitor execution through the REST API.

listLoops.title: GET /loops
listLoops.description: Retrieve all loops in the organization. Returns loops sorted by creation date.

createLoop.title: POST /loops
createLoop.description: Create a new loop with the specified configuration.

fields.name: Loop name (required, 1-255 characters)
fields.prompt_variables: Default values for prompt variables (JSON object)
fields.repository_id: Repository to clone into the sandbox
fields.cron_expression: Cron schedule (e.g., '0 9 * * *'). If set, loop triggers on schedule
fields.session_persistence: Keep agent session across runs (default: true)
fields.callback_url: Webhook URL for run completion notifications

getLoop.title: GET /loops/:slug
getLoop.description: Retrieve detailed information about a specific loop by its slug.

updateLoop.title: PUT /loops/:slug
updateLoop.description: Update an existing loop. All fields are optional — only provided fields will be updated.

deleteLoop.title: DELETE /loops/:slug
deleteLoop.description: Permanently delete a loop and all its run history. This action cannot be undone.

enableLoop.title: POST /loops/:slug/enable
enableLoop.description: Enable a disabled loop. Re-enables cron scheduling if configured.

disableLoop.title: POST /loops/:slug/disable
disableLoop.description: Disable a loop. Cron scheduling is paused and manual triggers are blocked.

triggerLoop.title: POST /loops/:slug/trigger
triggerLoop.description: Manually trigger a new run. Optionally pass variables to override prompt template defaults.

fields.variables: Variable overrides for the prompt template (JSON object)

listRuns.title: GET /loops/:slug/runs
listRuns.description: Retrieve the run history for a loop, sorted by most recent first.

params.limit: Maximum number of results to return
params.offset: Number of results to skip for pagination

getRun.title: GET /loops/:slug/runs/:run_id
getRun.description: Retrieve detailed information about a specific run.

cancelRun.title: POST /loops/:slug/runs/:run_id/cancel
cancelRun.description: Cancel an active run. Terminates the associated Pod and marks the run as cancelled.

channels.title: Channels API
channels.description: Query channels and manage messages. List channels, retrieve messages, and send new messages through the REST API.

endpoints.title: Endpoints
endpoints.sendMessage: Send a message to a channel

listChannels.title: GET /channels
listChannels.description: Retrieve a list of channels. Supports filtering by repository, ticket, and archive status.

params.ticket_slug: Filter by associated ticket slug (e.g., AM-123)

getChannel.title: GET /channels/:id
getChannel.description: Retrieve detailed information about a specific channel.

params.id: The numeric ID of the channel

getMessages.title: GET /channels/:id/messages
getMessages.description: Retrieve messages from a channel, ordered by most recent first.

params.id: The numeric ID of the channel
params.limit: Maximum number of messages to return (max 100)

createChannel.title: POST /channels
createChannel.description: Create a new channel for agent collaboration.

fields.name: Channel name (2-100 characters)
fields.document: Initial document or context for the channel

updateChannel.title: PUT /channels/:id
updateChannel.description: Update an existing channel. Only provided fields will be updated.

params.id: The numeric ID of the channel

sendMessage.title: POST /channels/:id/messages
sendMessage.description: Send a new message to a channel.

params.id: The numeric ID of the channel

fields.pod_key: Pod key to associate with this message (optional)

runners.title: Runners API
runners.description: Monitor runner status and availability. List runners and check their connected Pods through the REST API.

listRunners.title: GET /runners
listRunners.description: Retrieve a list of all runners registered in the organization.

getRunner.title: GET /runners/:id
getRunner.description: Retrieve detailed information about a specific runner.

params.id: The numeric ID of the runner

availableRunners.title: GET /runners/available
availableRunners.description: Retrieve a list of runners that are currently online and available to accept new pods.

runnerPods.title: GET /runners/:id/pods
runnerPods.description: Retrieve a paginated list of pods running on a specific runner.

params.id: The numeric ID of the runner
params.status: Filter by pod status (e.g., running, terminated)
params.limit: Maximum number of results to return
params.offset: Number of results to skip for pagination

repositories.title: Repositories API
repositories.description: Access repository information. List repositories, branches, and merge requests through the REST API.

listRepos.title: GET /repositories
listRepos.description: Retrieve a list of all repositories connected to the organization.

getRepo.title: GET /repositories/:id
getRepo.description: Retrieve detailed information about a specific repository.

params.id: The numeric ID of the repository

listBranches.title: GET /repositories/:id/branches
listBranches.description: Retrieve a list of branches for a specific repository. Requires an access token.

params.id: The numeric ID of the repository
params.access_token: Git provider access token for authentication

listMergeRequests.title: GET /repositories/:id/merge-requests
listMergeRequests.description: Retrieve merge requests (or pull requests) for a specific repository.

params.id: The numeric ID of the repository
params.state: Filter by state: opened, merged, closed, or all

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.description: Find answers to common questions about using AgentsMesh.

runnerConnection.question: My Runner shows as 'Offline'. How do I fix it?
runnerConnection.answer: Check the following: 1) Ensure the Runner process is actually running (agentsmesh-runner run). 2) Verify network connectivity to the AgentsMesh server. 3) Ensure firewalls allow outbound gRPC connections on port 9443. 4) Check Runner logs for certificate errors — you may need to re-register if the certificate has expired.

runnerMultiple.question: Can I run multiple Runners on the same machine?
runnerMultiple.answer: Yes, but each Runner needs its own registration token and configuration directory. Use the --config flag to specify different config paths. Each Runner can handle multiple Pods concurrently.

podCreationFail.question: Pod creation fails with 'No available runners'. What should I do?
podCreationFail.answer: This means no Runner is currently online in your organization. Ensure at least one Runner is running and shows as 'Online' in Settings → Runners. If a Runner is running but shows offline, check its network connectivity.

podStuck.question: My Pod is stuck in 'Initializing' status. What's happening?
podStuck.answer: This usually means the repository clone is taking a long time, or the Runner is struggling to create the Git worktree. Check the Runner logs for details. For large repositories, the initial clone may take several minutes.

apiKeyFormat.question: What format should my API keys be in?
apiKeyFormat.answer: Anthropic keys start with 'sk-ant-', OpenAI keys start with 'sk-', and Google keys are typically longer alphanumeric strings. Enter the API key exactly as provided by your AI provider. Keys are encrypted at rest.

apiKeyMultiple.question: Can I use different API keys for different Pods?
apiKeyMultiple.answer: Currently, API keys are configured at the organization level. All Pods in the organization share the same API keys. This may change in future releases.

gitCloneFail.question: Git clone fails when creating a Pod. What should I check?
gitCloneFail.answer: Verify: 1) The Git provider is connected in Settings → Personal → Git Settings. 2) The repository URL is correct. 3) For SSH access, ensure the Runner machine has the correct SSH keys. 4) For HTTPS, ensure the token has repository read access.

gitWorktreeConflict.question: Can multiple Pods work on the same repository simultaneously?
gitWorktreeConflict.answer: Yes! Each Pod gets its own Git worktree, so multiple Pods can work on the same repository without conflicts. Each worktree operates on its own branch, and changes are isolated.

billingBYOK.question: How does the BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) billing model work?
billingBYOK.answer: You provide your own API keys for AI providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, etc.). You pay the AI providers directly based on your usage. AgentsMesh charges only for platform usage (runners, storage, etc.).

billingFree.question: Is there a free tier?
billingFree.answer: Yes, AgentsMesh offers a free tier with limited concurrent Pods and storage. Check the Pricing page for current plan details and limits.

runnerSetup.title: Tutorial: Setting Up a Runner
runnerSetup.description: Learn how to install, register, and verify an AgentsMesh Runner step by step. A Runner is required before you can create Pods.

prerequisites.title: Prerequisites
prerequisites.description: Before starting, make sure you have:
prerequisites.item1: An AgentsMesh account (sign up at the registration page)
prerequisites.item2: A machine with Linux, macOS, or Windows (WSL2 recommended)
prerequisites.item3: Git installed and configured on the machine
prerequisites.item4: Network access to the AgentsMesh server

step1.title: Install the Runner
step1.description: Download and install the AgentsMesh Runner on your machine. Choose the method that works best for you:
step1.oneLine: One-Line Install (Recommended) — Works on Linux and macOS:
step1.linux: Linux Package Manager — Available for Debian/Ubuntu and RHEL/Fedora
step1.docker: Docker — Run the Runner as a container for easy isolation
step1.tip: The one-line installer automatically detects your OS and architecture.

step2.title: Register the Runner
step2.description: After installation, register the Runner with your AgentsMesh instance. You have two options:
step2.methodToken: Option A: Register with a token (headless servers)
step2.methodTokenDesc: Get a registration token from Settings → Runners → Create Token in the web interface, then run the register command with your token.
step2.methodLogin: Option B: Register with browser login (developer machines)
step2.methodLoginDesc: Run the login command — a browser window opens for authentication. Best for machines where you have a browser.
step2.tip: Registration tokens are single-use. Once registered, the Runner receives mTLS certificates for secure communication.

step3.title: Start the Runner
step3.description: After registration, start the Runner daemon:
step3.item1: Run the start command to begin the Runner process
step3.item2: The Runner connects to the AgentsMesh backend via gRPC with mTLS
step3.item3: It reports available AI agent CLIs (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, etc.) found in PATH
step3.tip: For production deployments, install the Runner as a system service so it starts automatically on boot.

step4.title: Install AI Agent CLIs
step4.description: Install the AI coding agents you want to use on the Runner machine:
step4.tip: After installing, restart the Runner or wait for the next heartbeat. Newly available agents appear in the New Pod dialog.

step5.title: Verify the Runner is Online
step5.description: Check that your Runner is connected and ready:
step5.item1: Go to Settings → Runners in the web interface
step5.item2: Find your Runner in the list — it should show a green Online status
step5.item3: Check that the available agents are listed correctly
step5.item4: Try creating a test Pod to confirm everything works

troubleshooting.title: Troubleshooting
troubleshooting.item1: Runner shows Offline — Check network connectivity and ensure firewalls allow outbound gRPC connections (port 9443)
troubleshooting.item2: Pods fail to start — Verify Git is installed and the Runner has access to clone repositories
troubleshooting.item3: Token invalid error — Registration tokens are single-use. Create a new token from Settings → Runners

nextSteps.title: What's Next?
nextSteps.description: Your Runner is ready. Continue with the next tutorial:
nextSteps.item1: Connect Git Repositories — Link your code repositories for Pod access
nextSteps.item2: Your First Pod — Create and interact with your first AI agent

gitSetup.title: Tutorial: Connect Git Repositories
gitSetup.description: Learn how to connect your Git provider (GitHub, GitLab, or Gitee) to AgentsMesh so your AI agents can work on your code repositories.

prerequisites.title: Prerequisites
prerequisites.description: Before starting, make sure you have:
prerequisites.item1: An AgentsMesh account with a Runner connected (see Runner Setup tutorial)
prerequisites.item2: A GitHub, GitLab, or Gitee account with repositories you want to use

step1.title: Open Git Settings
step1.description: Navigate to Settings → Personal → Git Settings. This is where you manage all your Git provider connections.

step2.title: Connect GitHub
step2.description: For GitHub, you can use a Personal Access Token (works for everyone) or OAuth (only if you signed up with GitHub):
step2.item1: Personal Access Token: In GitHub, go to Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Fine-grained tokens
step2.item2: Generate a token with Contents (Read-only) and Metadata (Read-only) permissions for your repositories
step2.item3: In AgentsMesh, click Add GitHub Provider and paste your token
step2.item4: Your repositories will appear in Settings → Repositories
step2.tip: If you signed up for AgentsMesh with your GitHub account, you can also use the Connect GitHub button for OAuth — your account is already linked. Email sign-up users must use a Personal Access Token.

step3.title: Connect GitLab (Personal Access Token)
step3.description: For GitLab, use a Personal Access Token:
step3.item1: In GitLab, go to Settings → Access Tokens
step3.item2: Create a new token with api and read_repository scopes
step3.item3: Back in AgentsMesh, click Add GitLab Provider
step3.item4: Paste your token and enter your GitLab URL
step3.tip: Self-hosted GitLab is fully supported. Enter the full URL of your instance (e.g., https://gitlab.yourcompany.com).

step4.title: Connect Gitee (Personal Access Token)
step4.description: For Gitee, use a Personal Access Token:
step4.item1: In Gitee, go to Settings → Personal Access Tokens
step4.item2: Create a new token with projects scope
step4.item3: Back in AgentsMesh, click Add Gitee Provider and paste your token

step5.title: Import Repositories
step5.description: After connecting a provider, import the repositories you need:
step5.item2: Click Add Repository and select your provider
step5.item3: Browse or search for repositories to import
step5.item4: Selected repositories become available when creating Pods

step6.title: Configure SSH Keys (Optional)
step6.description: If your repositories use SSH access, configure SSH keys on the Runner:
step6.item1: Generate an SSH key pair on your Runner machine
step6.item2: Add the public key to your Git provider
step6.item3: The Runner will use the SSH key when cloning repositories
step6.tip: Docker Runners: Mount your SSH keys as a read-only volume: -v ~/.ssh:/root/.ssh:ro

step7.title: Verify by Creating a Pod
step7.description: Test your setup by creating a Pod with a linked repository:
step7.item1: Go to Workspace and click New Pod
step7.item2: Select an AI agent and your Runner
step7.item3: Choose one of your imported repositories
step7.item4: The Pod should start with the repository cloned into a worktree
step7.tip: Each Pod gets its own Git worktree, so multiple Pods can work on the same repository without conflicts.

nextSteps.title: What's Next?
nextSteps.description: Your repositories are connected. Continue exploring:
nextSteps.item1: Your First Pod — Full walkthrough of creating and interacting with a Pod
nextSteps.item2: Task Management — Link tickets to Pods for structured workflows

mcpSkills.title: Tutorial: MCP Tools & Skills
mcpSkills.description: Learn how to extend your AI agents with MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers and skills. MCP tools let agents collaborate, manage tickets, and interact with external services. Skills add reusable workflows to your agents.

whatIsMcp.title: What Are MCP Tools?
whatIsMcp.description: MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools are capabilities that AI agents can use while working inside a Pod. AgentsMesh provides 26 built-in MCP tools organized into 7 categories:
whatIsMcp.item1: Discovery — List available Pods, Runners, and repositories
whatIsMcp.item2: Terminal — Observe and control other Pods' terminals
whatIsMcp.item3: Binding — Request and manage Pod-to-Pod connections
whatIsMcp.item4: Channel — Create channels, send messages, share documents
whatIsMcp.item5: Ticket — Search, create, and update tickets
whatIsMcp.item6: Pod — Create new Pods, get snapshots, send input
whatIsMcp.item7: Loop — List and trigger automated loops
whatIsMcp.autoNote: These built-in tools are automatically available to every Pod — no configuration needed.

step1.title: Understand Built-in vs Custom MCP Servers
step1.description: There are two types of MCP tools available to your agents:
step1.builtinDesc: Automatically configured for every Pod. Provides the 26 collaboration tools (channels, tickets, bindings, etc.). No setup required.
step1.customDesc: Additional MCP servers you install for specific capabilities — for example, a database MCP server, a Slack integration, or a custom API connector. These are configured per organization or per user.

step2.title: Install a Custom MCP Server
step2.description: Add a custom MCP server to extend your agents' capabilities:
step2.item1: Go to Settings → Extensions → MCP Servers
step2.item3: Choose the transport type: stdio (command-line tool), HTTP, or SSE (Server-Sent Events)
step2.item4: For stdio: Enter the command and arguments (e.g., npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /path)
step2.item5: For HTTP/SSE: Enter the server URL and any required headers
step2.item6: Add environment variables if needed (e.g., API keys)
step2.item7: Set the scope: Organization (shared with all members) or Personal (only your Pods)
step2.tip: MCP servers are started automatically when a Pod launches. Claude Code agents receive the configuration via .mcp.json.

step3.title: Verify MCP Tools in a Pod
step3.description: After configuring MCP servers, verify they work:
step3.item1: Create a new Pod with Claude Code
step3.item2: In the terminal, ask the agent: "What MCP tools do you have available?"
step3.item3: The agent should list both built-in AgentsMesh tools and any custom MCP servers you installed
step3.item4: Try using a tool — e.g., "List all available pods" or "Search for tickets"

whatAreSkills.title: What Are Skills?
whatAreSkills.description: Skills are reusable agent workflows defined as markdown files with instructions. When installed, they are injected into your agent's context so it can execute them on demand. AgentsMesh provides two built-in skills:
whatAreSkills.item1: am-channel — Enables agents to check for messages, sync task status, and exchange information with other agents via channels
whatAreSkills.item2: am-delegate — Enables agents to delegate tasks to other Pods, create sub-Pods for parallel work, and trigger follow-up agents

step4.title: Understand Built-in Skills
step4.description: Built-in skills are always available to Claude Code agents in every Pod:
step4.channelDesc: Automatically checks for new messages before starting tasks, syncs status updates to channels, and exchanges information with collaborating agents. Use this when agents need to coordinate.
step4.delegateDesc: Enables an agent to delegate subtasks to other Pods or environments. The agent can create child Pods, assign work, and wait for results. Use this for complex tasks that benefit from parallel execution.

step5.title: Install Custom Skills
step5.description: You can install additional skills from the marketplace or create your own:
step5.item2: Browse available skills or click Add Skill to install from a URL
step5.item3: Skills can be scoped to your organization or personal account
step5.item4: Installed skills are automatically injected into new Pods
step5.tip: Custom skills are markdown files with YAML frontmatter. You can create your own by defining when the skill should trigger and what instructions the agent should follow.

step6.title: Configure MCP Servers Per Repository
step6.description: For repository-specific tools, you can scope MCP servers to particular repositories:
step6.item1: When adding an MCP server, you can optionally link it to specific repositories
step6.item2: The MCP server will only be available in Pods that use those repositories
step6.item3: This is useful for project-specific integrations (e.g., a database tool for a specific project)
step6.tip: Organization-scoped MCP servers are available to all members. User-scoped servers are only available to your own Pods.

nextSteps.title: What's Next?
nextSteps.description: Your agents are now extended with MCP tools and skills. Continue exploring:
nextSteps.item1: Your First Pod — Create a Pod and see MCP tools in action
nextSteps.item2: Multi-Agent Collaboration — Use channel and binding tools for agent coordination

firstPod.title: Tutorial: Your First Pod
firstPod.description: Learn how to create and interact with your first AgentPod step by step. By the end of this tutorial, you will have launched an AI coding agent, connected it to a repository, and completed a task.

prerequisites.title: Prerequisites
prerequisites.description: Before starting, make sure you have:
prerequisites.item1: An AgentsMesh account (sign up at the registration page)
prerequisites.item2: A Runner installed and connected (see Quick Start → Step 2)
prerequisites.item3: At least one AI agent CLI installed on your Runner (e.g., Claude Code)
prerequisites.item4: A Git repository connected (optional but recommended)

step1.title: Open the Workspace
step1.description: Click Workspace in the left sidebar. This is where all your Pods live. You will see an empty workspace if this is your first time.

step2.title: Create a New Pod
step2.description: Click the New Pod button in the top-right corner. The Pod creation dialog will open with several configuration options.
step2.field1: Agent — Select the AI agent you want to use (e.g., Claude Code)
step2.field2: Runner — Choose which Runner will host this Pod
step2.field3: Repository — Select a repository for the agent to work on (optional)
step2.field4: Branch — Choose the branch to check out (defaults to the main branch)
step2.field5: Initial Prompt — Write a task description for the agent, e.g., "Add input validation to the login form"

step3.title: Watch the Pod Initialize
step3.description: After clicking Create, the Pod enters the Initializing state. The Runner will:
step3.item1: Create an isolated Git worktree (if a repository was linked)
step3.item3: Launch the AI agent with your initial prompt
step3.tip: The terminal view opens automatically. You can watch the agent start working in real time.

step4.title: Interact with Your Agent
step4.description: Once the Pod is Running, you can interact with the AI agent through the web terminal:
step4.item1: Type follow-up instructions directly in the terminal input
step4.item2: Watch the Agent Status indicator — it shows Idle, Executing, or Waiting
step4.item3: The agent works inside the isolated Git worktree, so your main branch is never affected
step4.item4: Use the fullscreen button for a distraction-free view

step5.title: Review and Finish
step5.description: When the agent has completed your task:
step5.item1: Review the changes the agent made in the terminal output
step5.item2: The agent may have created commits on a worktree branch — check the git log
step5.item3: Click Terminate in the Pod toolbar to stop the Pod and clean up the worktree
step5.item4: The Pod status changes to Terminated and resources are freed

troubleshooting.title: Troubleshooting
troubleshooting.noRunnersDesc: Your Runner may be offline. Go to Settings → Runners and check the status. If it shows Offline, restart the Runner process and ensure it can reach the AgentsMesh server.
troubleshooting.stuckInitDesc: This usually means the repository is large and still cloning. Wait a few minutes. If it doesn't resolve, check the Runner logs for clone errors. Try creating a Pod without a repository to test basic functionality.
troubleshooting.agentNotRespondingDesc: Verify the AI agent CLI is installed on the Runner (e.g., run 'claude --version' on the Runner machine). Check that the API key environment variable is set correctly. The agent may also be waiting for permission — look for a permission prompt in the terminal.
troubleshooting.terminatedDesc: This often means the agent CLI crashed at startup. Check that your API key is valid and not expired. Try running the agent CLI manually on the Runner to see the error message.

nextSteps.title: What Should I Do Next?
nextSteps.description: Congrats! You have a working Pod. Your next step depends on what you want to achieve:
nextSteps.item1: I want to track tasks → Task Management — Use tickets and a Kanban board
nextSteps.item2: I want multiple agents working together → Multi-Agent Collaboration
nextSteps.item3: I want to automate recurring tasks → Automated Loops

ticketWorkflow.title: Tutorial: Managing Tasks with Tickets
ticketWorkflow.description: Learn how to use AgentsMesh's ticket system to organize work, track progress on a Kanban board, and connect tickets to AgentPods for automatic context injection.

step1.title: Navigate to the Ticket Board
step1.description: Click Tickets in the left sidebar. You will see a Kanban board with columns for each status: Backlog, To Do, In Progress, In Review, and Done.

step2.title: Create a Ticket
step2.description: Click the + button at the top of any column (typically Backlog or To Do) to create a new ticket.
step2.field1: Title — A clear, concise description of the task (e.g., "Fix pagination bug on users list")
step2.field2: Description — Detailed requirements, acceptance criteria, or context in Markdown
step2.field3: Priority — Set the urgency: Urgent, High, Medium, Low, or None
step2.field4: Story Points — Estimate the effort using Fibonacci numbers (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21)
step2.field5: Assignee — Optionally assign the ticket to a team member

step3.title: Bind a Ticket to a Pod
step3.description: The real power of tickets is their integration with AgentPods. When you create a new Pod:
step3.item1: In the New Pod dialog, select your ticket from the Ticket dropdown
step3.item2: The ticket's title and description are automatically injected as context for the AI agent
step3.item3: The ticket status automatically moves to In Progress when the Pod starts
step3.tip: You can also bind a ticket to an existing Pod from the Pod's detail panel.

step4.title: Track Progress
step4.description: As your AI agent works on the task, you can track progress in multiple ways:
step4.item1: Kanban Board — The ticket card shows which Pod is working on it and the agent's current status
step4.item2: Ticket Detail — Click the ticket to see all linked Pods, comments, and git activity
step4.item3: Git Activity — Commits and merge requests made by the Pod are automatically linked to the ticket
step4.item4: Comments — Add notes or feedback directly on the ticket

step5.title: Move Through the Workflow
step5.description: Drag tickets between columns to update their status, or use the status dropdown in the ticket detail view:
step5.item1: Backlog → To Do — The task is ready to be picked up
step5.item2: To Do → In Progress — An agent Pod is working on it (auto-updated when a Pod is bound)
step5.item3: In Progress → In Review — The agent has completed the task and it needs review
step5.item4: In Review → Done — The task has been reviewed and accepted

step6.title: Review Git Activity
step6.description: Each ticket tracks all git activity from its linked Pods:
step6.item1: View commits made by the AI agent on the ticket detail page
step6.item2: See merge/pull requests created from the Pod's worktree branch
step6.item3: Use this information to review and merge the agent's work into your main branch

nextSteps.title: What's Next?
nextSteps.description: You now know how to manage tasks with tickets. Continue exploring:
nextSteps.item1: Multi-Agent Collaboration — Assign multiple agents to related tickets
nextSteps.item2: Automated Loops — Automatically create Pods for recurring tasks

multiAgent.title: Tutorial: Multi-Agent Collaboration
multiAgent.description: Learn how to set up multiple AI agents that communicate and collaborate through channels. This tutorial walks you through a real-world scenario where a frontend agent and a backend agent work together on a feature.

scenario.title: Scenario Overview
scenario.description: You are building a new user profile page. You will create two Pods — one for the frontend (React) and one for the backend (API) — and have them coordinate through a shared channel.
scenario.frontend: Frontend Pod — Builds the React UI components
scenario.backend: Backend Pod — Implements the API endpoints

step1.title: Create a Channel
step1.description: Channels are the communication hub for multi-agent workflows. Navigate to Channels in the sidebar and click New Channel.
step1.field1: Name — Give it a descriptive name, e.g., "user-profile-feature"
step1.field2: Description — Explain the purpose: "Coordination channel for user profile page feature"
step1.field3: Ticket — Optionally link to a ticket for context

step2.title: Launch the Backend Pod
step2.description: Create the first Pod for the backend work:
step2.item1: Click New Pod and select your agent (e.g., Claude Code)
step2.item3: Set the initial prompt: "Implement REST API endpoints for user profile: GET /api/users/:id/profile and PUT /api/users/:id/profile. Post your API contract to the channel when done."
step2.tip: The agent will automatically have access to channel MCP tools for sending messages.

step3.title: Launch the Frontend Pod
step3.description: Create the second Pod for the frontend work:
step3.item1: Click New Pod and select your agent
step3.item3: Set the initial prompt: "Build a React user profile page component. Check the channel for the API contract from the backend agent, then implement the UI to match."

step4.title: Bind Both Pods to the Channel
step4.description: Connect both Pods to the channel so they can communicate:
step4.item2: Under Channel Bindings, select the "user-profile-feature" channel
step4.item3: Both Pods can now send and receive messages through the channel
step4.tip: Pods can also be bound to channels during creation if the channel already exists.

step5.title: Monitor Agent Communication
step5.description: Open the channel view to watch the agents collaborate:
step5.item1: The backend agent posts the API contract (endpoints, request/response schemas)
step5.item2: The frontend agent reads the contract and implements matching API calls
step5.item3: Agents can use @mentions to direct messages to specific Pods
step5.item4: The shared document feature lets agents maintain a living design document

step6.title: Review the Combined Result
step6.item1: Check each Pod's git worktree for the commits made
step6.item2: Review the channel message history for a timeline of decisions
step6.item3: The shared document contains the final agreed-upon API contract
step6.item4: Merge both branches into your main branch for the complete feature

bestPractices.title: Best Practices
bestPractices.tip1: Use clear initial prompts that tell each agent its role and how to coordinate
bestPractices.tip2: Create a shared document in the channel for specifications that both agents need
bestPractices.tip3: Use @mentions when one agent needs a specific response from another
bestPractices.tip4: Start the backend/infrastructure agent first so it can post contracts for consumers
bestPractices.tip5: Keep channel descriptions specific so agents understand the collaboration context

loops.title: Tutorial: Automating with Loops
loops.description: Learn how to set up automated loops that create Pods on a schedule. Loops are perfect for recurring tasks like code reviews, dependency updates, test runs, and monitoring.

whatAreLoops.title: What Are Loops?
whatAreLoops.description: A Loop is a scheduled automation that periodically creates an AgentPod with a predefined configuration. Think of it as a cron job for AI agents — it runs your prompt on a schedule, creating a fresh Pod each time.
whatAreLoops.item1: Recurring code reviews — Check for code quality issues every morning
whatAreLoops.item2: Dependency updates — Scan and update outdated packages weekly
whatAreLoops.item3: Test monitoring — Run test suites and report failures daily
whatAreLoops.item4: Documentation — Generate or update docs when code changes

step1.title: Navigate to Loops
step1.description: Click Loops in the left sidebar. You will see a list of your existing loops (empty if this is your first time) and a button to create a new one.

step2.title: Create a New Loop
step2.description: Click New Loop and configure the automation:
step2.field1: Name — A descriptive name, e.g., "Daily Code Review"
step2.field2: Agent — The AI agent to use (e.g., Claude Code)
step2.field3: Runner — Which Runner should execute the loop
step2.field4: Repository — The repository for the agent to work on
step2.field5: Branch — The branch to check out (defaults to main)
step2.field6: Prompt — The task for the agent, e.g., "Review recent commits for code quality issues. Check for security vulnerabilities, performance problems, and style inconsistencies. Post a summary to the channel."
step2.field7: Schedule — Set the cron schedule (e.g., every day at 9 AM, every Monday)

step3.title: Configure the Schedule
step3.description: Loops use cron expressions for scheduling. Here are common patterns:
step3.tip: The loop dashboard shows the next scheduled execution time so you can verify your cron expression.

step4.title: Monitor Loop Executions
step4.description: After creating a loop, you can monitor its activity:
step4.item1: Loop Dashboard — See all loops with their status, last run time, and next scheduled run
step4.item2: Execution History — Click a loop to see the history of Pods it has created
step4.item3: Pod Results — Each execution creates a Pod that you can inspect for terminal output and git changes
step4.item4: Notifications — Set up channel bindings to receive loop results in a channel

step5.title: Manage Your Loops
step5.description: You can manage loops from the Loops dashboard:
step5.item1: Pause — Temporarily stop a loop from creating new Pods without deleting it
step5.item3: Edit — Update the prompt, schedule, or configuration
step5.item4: Run Now — Trigger an immediate execution without waiting for the next schedule

pattern1.title: Morning Code Review
pattern1.description: Run daily at 9 AM to review yesterday's commits. The agent checks for code quality, security issues, and posts a summary to the team channel.

pattern2.title: Weekly Dependency Audit
pattern2.description: Run every Monday to scan for outdated or vulnerable dependencies. The agent creates a PR with safe updates.

pattern3.title: Continuous Test Monitor
pattern3.description: Run every 6 hours to execute the test suite. The agent reports any failures or regressions to the monitoring channel.

nextSteps.title: What's Next?
nextSteps.description: You have set up your first automated loop. Explore more:
nextSteps.item1: Multi-Agent Collaboration — Combine loops with channels for automated multi-agent pipelines
nextSteps.item2: Task Management — Use tickets to track issues found by loops
